data.title: Treasury Yields Extend Decline Amid Stale Data — Market Talk
data.description: Treasury yields slip as the U.S. government releases data delayed by last fall's long shutdown. Producer price increases accelerated 0.2% in November from 0.1% in October. Economists surveyed by WSJ expected a 0.3% pace in November. Also in November, retail sales grew 0.6%, versus 0.4% consensus and after declining 0.1% in October. Tomorrow, weekly jobless claims are expected to increase to 215,000 from 208,000. Geopolitical tensions and President Trump's attacks on Fed Chair Powell help boost demand for Treasurys, some analysts say. The 10-year yield is at 4.156% and the two-year at 3.518%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Retail Sales Brightened in November
data.description:
By Matt Grossman

Sales growth for U.S. retailers picked up in November, evidence that the consumer economy didn't lose steam in the home stretch of last year.

Retail sales grew by 0.6% in November to $735.9 billion, an improvement from a slight pullback by shoppers in October, the Commerce Department said Wednesday. Analysts polled by The Wall Street Journal were expecting a 0.4% November increase.

In some ways, the economy now looks more fragile than during the post-pandemic economic boom, with more slack in the labor market and heightened uncertainty over changes in federal trade and immigration policy. Yet personal spending, which makes up more than two-thirds of the overall economy, hasn't missed a beat.

Americans' penchant to keep on shopping has contributed to many economists' upbeat forecasts for 2026, along with favorable tax-code changes and expectations of less policy whiplash in the second year of the Trump administration's second term. In December, Federal Reserve policymakers penciled in an uptick in growth this year compared with 2025, an outlook shared by many Wall Street prognosticators.

The November retail-sales figures are landing after a delay caused by last year's government shutdown, which threw a wrench into the federal statistics' agencies efforts to gather data on the economy. The Census Bureau, the arm of Commerce that collects retail statistics, is still catching up on missed reports, with some data series now lagging the economy by three months.

Write to Matt Grossman at matt.grossman@wsj.com

data.title: Norwegian Krone Could Rise on Strong Fundamentals — Market Talk
data.description: The Norwegian krone looks on course to strengthen further against the euro as Norway's economy demonstrates superior fundamentals compared to the euro area, RBC Capital Markets strategists say in a note. This includes consistent productivity gains, real wage growth and a rare combination of strong trend growth with elevated nominal yields, they say. The Norges Bank maintains the G-10's highest policy rate at 4% with limited appetite for rate cuts given a resilient labor market and high inflation. Norway's one-year swap rates exceed Sweden's by over 200 basis points, they say. RBC recommends selling the euro versus the krone with a target of 11.25 krone and stop loss of 12.00. The euro falls 0.1% to 11.7268 krone. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Spot Restoration Announces 24/7 Water Damage Restoration Services in Miami
data.description:
Miami, Florida--(Newsfile Corp. - January 14, 2026) - Spot Restoration, a Miami-based restoration company, has announced the availability of its 24/7 water damage restoration services for residential and commercial properties across the Miami area.

To view an enhanced version of this graphic, please visit:

https://images.newsfilecorp.com/files/8530/280339_aee36fe404193a6e_001full.jpg

The company provides water damage restoration, flood cleanup, structural drying, and mold remediation services designed to address both minor and large-scale property damage. Spot Restoration utilizes industry-standard equipment and established remediation procedures to support efficient property recovery following water-related incidents.

According to the company, the service offering is intended to support property owners who require timely response and professional assistance during water damage emergencies. Spot Restoration's restoration process includes inspection, moisture control, cleanup, and remediation to help return properties to safe and functional conditions.

In addition to water damage restoration, Spot Restoration offers mold remediation services aimed at addressing contamination that may develop after moisture exposure. The company emphasizes clear communication and professional service throughout the restoration process.

Spot Restoration currently serves Miami and surrounding areas, providing around-the-clock availability to respond to water damage situations as they arise.

About Spot Restoration

Spot Restoration is a Miami-based water damage restoration company offering 24/7 emergency services, including water extraction, flood cleanup, structural drying, and mold remediation for residential and commercial properties.

Chief of Media & Marketing

data.title: Wholesale-cost increases during government shutdown point to persistent inflation in U.S. economy
data.description:
By Jeffry Bartash

Producer price index has risen 3% in the past year

Wholesale prices rose during the government shutdown and signaled persistent inflation.

The cost of wholesale goods and services rose during the government shutdown and showed the persistence of inflation pressures in the guts of the U.S. economy.

A combined report on producer prices showed a 0.2% increase in November and a 0.1% rise in October, the government said. The two months were combined due to the recent government shutdown.

The 12-month increase in wholesale prices - where inflation tends to show up first - also climbed to 3% from 2.8%.

The PPI survey was somewhat incomplete, however, because government statisticians were unable to collect all the price data they normally do during the 43-day shutdown. Economists say it make take a few more months to get a more accurate read on inflation trends.

The core rate of inflation rose 0.2% in November and a steeper 0.7% in October.

The 12-month increase in core prices edged up to 3.5% in November from 3.4% in the prior month and matched a nine-month high.

The core rate strips out volatile food, energy and trade-related costs and gives a more accurate view of inflation trends.

Key details: The yearly rate of wholesale inflation is still too high, but price increases appeared to have stabilized around a 3% rate.

Wholesale prices reflect the costs companies pay for supplies or for products they intend to sell directly to consumers. When these prices change, it usually influences the rate of inflation.

Big picture: The government shutdown in October and November is still clouding key economic reports and making it hard to glean the latest trends in inflation.

The good news is, price increases appear to have relaxed since the summer. The bad news? The rate of inflation is still running above what is considered a health level for the economy.

The Fed is trying to bring the rate of inflation down to 2% from current levels closer to 3%.

Market reaction: The Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA and S&P 500 SPX were set to decline in Wednesday trading.

-Jeffry Bartash

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: French Sovereign CDS Costs Rise Amid Weak Public Finances — Market Talk
data.description: The cost of default protection of French sovereign bonds edges higher as the country's public finances look in a weak state. The French government budget deficit of 155.4 billion euros in November, published this week, was at the higher end of expectations, Deutsche Bank Research strategists say in a note. "Our economists expect the overall 2025 budget deficit to reach 5.6% of GDP, two-tenths above the government's earlier target," they say. The French 5-year credit default swap spreads rise one basis point to last trade at 30bps, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: Wholesale Prices Rose Less Than Expected in November
data.description:
By Matt Grossman

Prices charged by goods- and services-producing companies increased in November, but the month's wholesale inflation was more modest than economists had expected.

The producer price index increased by 0.2% in November, and by 0.1% in October, according to a delayed Labor Department report that combined the two months' data after last year's government shutdown pushed back publication.

Analysts polled by The Wall Street Journal had expected a 0.3% month-over-month November increase.

Economists typically watch the PPI numbers closely because some of the underlying data is used in calculating the personal-consumption expenditures price index, the inflation metric that the Federal Reserve uses to gauge progress against its 2% inflation target.

The government-shutdown-related delays have thrown the data calendar out of sync, however, diminishing the immediate value of Wednesday's report.

Data released by the Labor Department Tuesday showed that consumer prices climbed by 2.7% in the 12 months through December. The next PPI inflation report, due Jan. 30, will give economists most of the data they will need to accurately estimate December's PCE inflation reading--before the official December number lands on Feb. 20.

Write to Matt Grossman at matt.grossman@wsj.com

data.description:

(13:22 GMT) *OPEC Forecasts 2027 Non-DoC Supply Growth at 610,000 B/D
(13:26 GMT) *OPEC Crude Output Rose by 105,000 B/D in December to 28.56 Mln B/D, OPEC Says, Citing Secondary Sources
(13:26 GMT) *Venezuela Crude Output Fell by 60,000 B/D in December to 896,000 B/D, OPEC Says, Citing Secondary Sources
(13:49 GMT) OPEC's First Look at 2027 Signals Steady Oil-Demand Growth

By Giulia Petroni

The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries expects world oil demand to grow at a steady pace in 2027 in its first outlook for the year.

The Vienna-based cartel projects demand will rise by 1.34 million barrels a day next year, slightly below 2026's estimated growth of 1.38 million barrels a day, according to its closely watched monthly report.

Supply from producers outside the wider OPEC+ alliance is expected to rise by 610,000 barrels a day in 2027, slightly below this year's estimated 630,000 barrels a day, driven by Brazil, Canada, Qatar, and Argentina.

In December, OPEC crude-oil output rose by 105,000 barrels a day to 28.56 million barrels a day, while total production from OPEC+ members fell by 238,000 barrels a day to 42.83 million barrels a day, weighed down by a significant decline in Kazakhstan's output.

Write to Giulia Petroni at giulia.petroni@wsj.com

data.title: Airbnb Appoints Former Meta AI Executive CTO
data.description:
By Nicholas G. Miller

Airbnb named Ahmad Al-Dahle, a former leader of AI at Meta, as the company's new chief technology officer.

Al-Dahle led generative AI at Meta, where he headed the team behind Meta's Llama AI models. He also previously worked at Apple, helping to develop the iPhone's display and multitouch systems and the AI systems for the company's self-driving car project.

Airbnb said the addition of Al-Dahle would help it "shape how people travel, live, and interact with AI in a way that strengthens human connection."

"He connects big ideas with technical depth, highly values design, and believes engineering should be a true strategic partner in everything we do," said Chief Executive Brian Chesky.

Airbnb is currently implementing AI agents into its business to improve customer service, personalize recommendations and facilitate trip planning.

Write to Nicholas G. Miller at nicholas.miller@wsj.com.

data.title: Ireland raises 5 billion euros from 10-year bond amid bumper demand
data.description:
Key points:
  • Ireland raises more than expected amid high demand
  • New benchmark bond sold at yield of 3.145%

Ireland raised 5 billion euros ($5.8 billion) from the sale of a new 10-year bond on Wednesday, a lead manager on the deal said, covering up to half of the country's annual funding needs in one go thanks to bumper demand.

Ireland's debt agency, which plans to raise a relatively modest 10 billion to 14 billion euros this year due to the government projecting another budget surplus, sold the new bond via a syndicate of banks and brokers at a yield of 3.145%.

Ireland's National Treasury Management Agency (NTMA) had targeted around 4 billion euros from the sale, a market source told Reuters on Tuesday. But it sold more than that after receiving 43 billion euros worth of orders for the security.

The NTMA, like some other European debt management offices, typically raises a chunk of funds early in the year by selling bonds through syndication.

Greece attracted a record 51 billion euros in orders when it sold 4 billion euros of a 10-year syndicated bond at a yield of 3.47% on Tuesday.

Like Ireland, Greece's economy is outperforming most of its European peers. Both countries were locked out of debt markets over a decade ago and had to seek financial bailouts.

Barclays, BofA Securities, Cantor Fitzgerald Ireland, Goodbody, J.P. Morgan and NatWest were the joint lead managers for Ireland's sale on Wednesday.

($1 = 0.8586 euros)

data.title: Central bank ability matters more than autonomy
data.description:
By Jon Sindreu

The heads of 11 monetary authorities rallied around Fed Chair Jerome Powell following Donald Trump’s attacks. Yet political oversight of interest rates does not necessarily lead to hyperinflation. The big risk is that undermining experts makes them less able to respond to crises.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow Jon Sindreu on X and LinkedIn.

CONTEXT NEWS

On January 13, the heads of 11 central banks around the world issued a ‌joint statement in support of Jerome Powell after the Trump ‌administration threatened the Federal Reserve chair with a ​criminal indictment. The U.S. administration's criminal probe is ‍formally about the renovation of the Fed's headquarters but on January 12 Powell called it a pretext to intimidate the central bank into lowering interest rates.

data.title: Citigroup rises after Q4 results
data.description:
** Citigroup's shares rise 1.7% to $118.30 premarket

** Co's adjusted Q4 profit per share of $1.81 beats Street estimates of $1.67 - data compiled by LSEG

** C takes $1.2 billion charge tied to sale of Russia business to Renaissance Capital, which was approved by its board last month

** Stock rose over 65% in 2025

data.title: PVH Corp Coverage Assumed by Jefferies at Buy
data.description: (13:42 GMT) PVH Corp Price Target Cut to $85.00/Share From $95.00 by Jefferies

data.title: Jyske Bank Shares Jump on Earnings Beat
data.description:
By Dominic Chopping

Jyske Bank shares jumped after the Danish bank said it expects full-year earnings to land above guidance.

Shares were 6.8% higher in afternoon Copenhagen trade.

The company had guided to net profit of between 4.9 billion and 5.3 billion Danish kroner ($763.5 million-$825.8 million) in 2025, but said in an earnings pre-release Wednesday that it now expects it ended the year with net profit at 5.41 billion kroner.

Earnings per share for the year is expected at 85 kroner versus guidance of between 77 to 84 kroner.

"The improved outlook is driven by a broadly based positive development, supported by a high level of activity and favorable financial markets," the bank said in a statement.

Fourth-quarter core earnings landed at 1.83 billion kroner, which is 10% ahead of consensus, while adjusted for one-offs in net interest income and costs the beat is 20%, UBS analyst Johan Ekblom said in a note.

Ekblom said the beat is driven by strong revenue generation and lower expenses than expected when adjusted for one-offs.

"While we would be cautious in fully extrapolating the Q4 trends before seeing the full details due on February 5, we...see scope for consensus EPS upgrades," he said.

Write to Dominic Chopping at dominic.chopping@wsj.com
